sanskrit_devanagari:
प्राप्य पुण्यकृतां लोकानुषित्वा शाश्वतीः समाः ।
शुचीनां श्रीमतां गेहे योगभ्रष्टोऽभिजायते ॥ ४१ ॥

śucīnāṁ śrīmatāṁ gehe yoga-bhraṣṭo 'bhijāyate || 41 ||

anvaya.meaning: of those who have done meritorious deeds

translations.verbatim_quote: The unsuccessful yogī, after many, many years of enjoyment on the planets of the pious living entities, is born into a family of righteous people, or into a family of rich aristocracy.
The unsuccessful yogi, after many, many years of enjoyment on the planets of the pious living entities, is born into a family of righteous people, or into a family of rich aristocracy.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1002 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The unsuccessful yogīs are divided into two classes: one is fallen after very little progress, and one is fallen after long practice of yoga. The yogī who falls after a short period of practice goes to the higher planets, where pious living entities are allowed to enter.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.41 This person, who had wandered away from Yoga because of desire for whatever kind of enjoyments, he will gain those very enjoyments through the excellence of Yoga alone. Having attained to the worlds of those who do meritorious acts, he will dwell there for a long time, i.e., till his

disagreements_among_translators.word: puṇya-kṛtāṁ lokān ... śucīnāṁ śrīmatāṁ gehe (the worlds of the meritorious / the home of the pure and prosperous)

positions.rendering: the unsuccessful yogi enjoys the planets of the pious for many years and then takes birth in a family of righteous people, or in a family of rich aristocracy
reaches worlds attained by performers of meritorious deeds, dwells for innumerable years, then reborn in the home of the pure and prosperous where the practice can be resumed
attains heavens won by accumulated puṇya, dwells until exhausted, then born in family of pure and prosperous where conditions favour resumption of yoga

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings preserve the trans-life trajectory. The verse is metaphysically loaded; the engineering analog operates at single-life single-career scope and is honest about the scope-mismatch.

literal_meaning: Having reached the worlds of those who have done meritorious deeds, having dwelt there for endless years, the one fallen from yoga is born in the home of the pure and prosperous.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.41 is the first half of the compounding-of-practice answer. The fallen yogi reaches puṇya-kṛtām lokān (the worlds of those who have done puṇya — merit), dwells there śāśvatīḥ samāḥ (for many years), and then is reborn in the home of the śuci (pure) and śrīmat (prosperous, blessed with prosperity). Sankara's reading: the fallen yogi reaches the worlds of those who have performed meritorious deeds, dwells there for innumerable years, and is then reborn in the home of the pure and the prosperous, where he can resume the practice from his prior level. Ramanuja's reading: the fallen yogi attains the heavens won by the puṇya he has accumulated, dwells there until that puṇya is exhausted, and is then born in a family of pure and prosperous people, where the conditions for resuming the yoga are favourable. The verse is metaphysically loaded — it explicitly describes trans-life trajectory. The engineering analog operates at single-life scope: the prior practice creates conditions favourable to resumption. STRETCHED honestly. The trans-life claim is preserved at source-pack level.
